11: The kᵉḇôḏ YHWH filling the temple at its dedication — wᵉkāḇôḏ YHWH mālaʾ ʾet bêt YHWH — is the OT’s type of the divine presence fully inhabiting a human-constructed dwelling. John 1:14 is the NT’s answer to what this presence ultimately was: kai ho logos sarx egeneto kai eskēnōsen en hēmin, kai etheasametha tēn doxan autou — ‘the Word became flesh and tabernacled (eskēnōsen) among us, and we beheld his glory.’ The verb skēnoō (to pitch a tent, to tabernacle) directly evokes the Tent of Meeting and the temple tradition: the divine glory that filled Solomon’s temple at dedication now inhabits human flesh at the incarnation. The Johannine word ‘glory’ (doxa) translates the Hebrew kāḇôḏ — John is saying that what Israel saw in the cloud-filled temple, the disciples saw in the person of Jesus. Revelation 21:3 (‘the dwelling of God is with man, and he will dwell with them’) is the eschatological consummation: the presence that filled the Solomonic temple, that tabernacled in Christ, will fill the new creation without mediation. The typological sequence — tabernacle (Exod 40) → temple (1 Kgs 8) → incarnation (John 1) → new creation (Rev 21) — traces the single trajectory of divine inresidence seeking its final home.
27: Solomon’s prayer — hinnēh haššāmayim ûšᵉmê haššāmayim lōʾ yᵉḵalᵉlûḵāh ʾap kî habbayit hazzeh, ‘heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you, how much less this house’ — poses the exact problem that the incarnation resolves. The Deuteronomic šēm-theology was a partial solution: YHWH’s name dwells there, not YHWH’s person — divine accessibility without divine containment. Stephen applies this in Acts 7:48-49 (‘the Most High does not dwell in houses made by hands’) to argue the temple’s obsolescence. But the NT’s resolution is not the temple’s abolition — it is the incarnation as the one place where the divine name and the divine person truly coincide. Jesus in John 2:19-21 responds to the demand for a temple-sign: ‘destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up’ — and the narrator explains: ekeinos de elegen peri tou naou tou sōmatos autou, ‘he was speaking about the temple of his body.’ The house Solomon said could not contain YHWH has its answer in the body of the Son: the one in whom ‘all the fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily’ (Colossians 2:9) — who does not merely bear the divine name but is the Word who was with God and was God (John 1:1), now in flesh.
30: The dedicatory prayer’s repeated refrain — wᵉʾattāh tišmaʿ ʾel mᵉqôm šiḇtᵉḵā ʾel haššāmayim wᵉšāmaʿtā wᵉsālaḥtā, ‘and you will hear in your dwelling place in heaven, and when you hear you will forgive’ — establishes the temple as the mediation-point for divine forgiveness: prayer directed toward the house ascends to YHWH enthroned in heaven, who hears and forgives. Hebrews 7:25 names the fulfillment: hothen kai sōzein eis to panteles dynatai tous proserchomenous di’ autou tō theō, pantote zōn eis to entygchanein hyper autōn — ‘therefore he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them.’ The temple’s directional prayer-structure (‘toward this house... YHWH will hear in heaven’) becomes in Heb 7 a person-structure: Jesus himself is the mediating point through whom prayer ascends and forgiveness descends, and he lives permanently to make that intercession. Romans 8:34 makes the same point: hos kai entygchanei hyper hēmōn, ‘he who also intercedes for us.’ The temple’s mediation was architectural and ritual; Christ’s mediation is personal and unending — the fulfillment and replacement of everything the temple’s architecture was designed to express.
1: Solomon assembles all Israel to Jerusalem to bring up the ark — the gathering of the whole community around the presence of God. Christ gathers a greater assembly: “where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them” (Matthew 18:20). The ark-procession to the temple is the type whose antitype is Christ himself drawing the nations to himself (John 12:32).
2: The feast in the seventh month, the month of Ethanim (Tishri), coincides with the Festival of Booths/Tabernacles — the great harvest feast when Israel lived in temporary shelters recalling wilderness sojourn. John 7:2-10 records Jesus at the Festival of Tabernacles, identifying himself as the living water the feast symbolized (John 7:37-38). The dedication of the temple at Tabernacles creates the typological backdrop for Jesus’s Tabernacles-season revelation of himself as the true water and light.
3: The priests carry the ark to its place — a priestly act of installation, of bringing the divine presence into the holy place. Christ enters the greater and more perfect tent “not made with hands” (Hebrews 9:11), installing not a wooden box containing the covenant but his own flesh as the new locus of divine-human meeting.
4: The ark of the LORD, the tent of meeting, and all the sacred vessels are brought up together. All the holy structures of the wilderness period converge at the temple — the mobility of the tabernacle succeeded by the permanence of the temple. Christ fulfills both poles: he is the tent (John 1:14, eskēnōsen — he pitched his tent/tabernacled) and the permanent dwelling (Colossians 2:9 — the fullness of deity dwells bodily in him).
5: Sacrifices too numerous to count as the ark is carried in — an expression of the abundance of Israel’s worship at this supreme moment. Christ’s one offering of himself (Hebrews 10:14) is the single sacrifice that ends the uncountable sacrificial multitude: “by one sacrifice he has made perfect forever those who are being made holy.”
6: The priests bring the ark to the Most Holy Place (debir) under the wings of the cherubim. The ark of the covenant — with its mercy seat (kapporet) where blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement — enters the innermost sanctuary. Paul calls Christ hilastērion in Romans 3:25 — the mercy seat, the place of atonement. What the ark and its cover typified, Christ fulfills: God’s presence meeting sinners through atoning blood in the holiest place.
7: The cherubim spread their wings over the ark, sheltering it. The cherubim mark the place of God’s enthroned presence (Exodus 25:22: “there I will meet with you”). Christ is the one in whom God meets humanity; the place “between the cherubim” is fulfilled in the incarnate Son in whom “all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell” (Colossians 1:19).
8: The poles of the ark extended so their tips were visible from the Holy Place — visible but not seen from outside. The divine presence is discernible to those who draw near, concealed from the casual observer. Jesus speaks in parables precisely for this reason: “to you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom; to them it has not been given” (Matthew 13:11). Access to the presence requires drawing near, as the poles require entering the Holy Place to see them.
9: The ark contained nothing except the two stone tablets from Horeb — the covenant law. The ark as the container of the law is a type of Christ as the embodiment of the law: “Do not think I have come to abolish the Law — I have come to fulfill it” (Matthew 5:17). What the ark housed in stone, Christ internalized in flesh. Hebrews 10:7 quotes Psalms 40:8: “in the scroll of the book it is written of me — I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart.”
10: When the priests came out of the Holy Place, a cloud filled the house of the LORD. The cloud is the shekinah — the visible manifestation of divine presence (the pillar of cloud in the wilderness, Exodus 40:34-38). John 1:14: “The Word became flesh and eskēnōsen among us, and we beheld his glory.” The cloud-filled temple is the OT form of what the Incarnation fulfills: God’s glory dwelling among his people in visible, tangible presence.
12: Solomon declares: “The LORD has declared he would dwell in thick darkness (araphel).” The araphel — the dense cloud of divine presence (Exodus 20:21; Deuteronomy 5:22) — is the inaccessible glory of God that no one can approach and live. But Christ makes the inaccessible accessible: “No one has ever seen God; the only Son, who is at the Father’s side, has made him known” (John 1:18). The thick darkness is pierced by the light of the incarnate Word.
13: Solomon has built an “exalted house (beit zebul) — a permanent dwelling place.” The temple is intended as a permanent habitation. But Jesus declared the greater permanence: “Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away” (Matthew 24:35). The temple Solomon built was destroyed; the dwelling Christ establishes — in the hearts of believers by the Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16; 1 Kings 6:19) — is the permanent fulfillment the temple foreshadowed.
14: The king turns and blesses the whole assembly — a priestly-royal act of benediction. Christ blesses his disciples as he ascends (Luke 24:50-51) — the final act of the incarnate Lord as he departs is a gesture of blessing toward his people. Solomon’s blessing of the assembly before the temple is a type of the ascending Christ’s benediction before the heavenly sanctuary.
15: Solomon blesses the LORD who “spoke with his own mouth to my father David and fulfilled it with his own hand.” The pattern of divine word → divine fulfillment runs through all Scripture and finds its climax in Christ, who is himself the Word (John 1:1) that God spoke and fulfilled simultaneously. Every promise made through the prophets is “Yes” in him (2 Corinthians 1:20).
16: God chose no city among the tribes for a house — but he chose David. The election of a person (David) over a place precedes and shapes the election of a place (Jerusalem). The NT sharpens this: God has chosen a person, not primarily a place — “you are a royal priesthood” gathered around the risen Christ, not a building (1 Peter 2:9). The final form of God’s presence is not in Jerusalem’s stones but in the living community gathered around Christ.
17: It was in David’s heart to build a house for the LORD’s name. The desire was good, though David was not the appointed builder. The longing for God’s dwelling among his people is the deep current of OT piety — from the tabernacle to the temple to the prophetic hope (Ezekiel 48:35: “The LORD is There”). Christ is the fullest answer to David’s desire: “The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.”
18: God affirmed David’s desire: “You did well to have it in your heart.” The desire for God’s presence and glory is itself honored, even when the form of fulfillment differs from what was imagined. Christ honors the genuine longing of all OT worship: “Your father Abraham rejoiced that he would see my day — he saw it and was glad” (John 8:56). The temple-longing David felt was longing for Christ, though he did not yet know the name.
19: David shall not build — “your son shall build the house for my name.” The Son builds what the Father prepared: the Davidic Son (Solomon) builds the earthly temple; the eternal Son (Christ) builds the heavenly and spiritual temple. Hebrews 3:3-6: “Moses was faithful as a servant in all God’s house, but Christ is faithful as a son over God’s house. And we are his house.”
20: The LORD has kept the promise — Solomon sits on David’s throne and has built the temple. Fulfilled promise is the grammar of redemptive history; each fulfillment points toward and is surpassed by the final fulfillment in Christ. The throne David held and Solomon inherited finds its ultimate occupant in the one of whom Gabriel says: “The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over the house of Jacob forever” (Luke 1:32-33).
21: Solomon has provided a place for the ark — the covenant between God and Israel now has a permanent dwelling. Christ is the new covenant’s dwelling; Hebrews 9:15 calls him “the mediator of a new covenant.” The ark housed the old covenant tablets; Christ inaugurates the new covenant in his blood (Luke 22:20), and the law is now written on hearts (Jeremiah 31:33; Hebrews 10:16) rather than stone.
22: Solomon stands before the altar of the LORD before the whole assembly, hands spread toward heaven — the posture of priestly intercession. The high-priestly prayer in John 17 is the NT parallel: Jesus “lifted up his eyes to heaven” (John 17:1) and interceded for his people before the cross. Solomon’s posture of intercession is the type; Christ’s eternal intercession (Hebrews 7:25 — “he always lives to make intercession for them”) is the antitype.
23: “There is no God like you in heaven above or on earth below — you keep covenant and steadfast love (hesed) for your servants.” The incomparability of YHWH (there is no God like him) is the ground of all intercession. In Christ, this incomparable God becomes personally present among his people: “For in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell” (Colossians 1:19). The hesed that is God’s character is enacted in Christ’s incarnation, death, and resurrection.
24: The prayer grounds itself in fulfilled promise: “What you spoke with your mouth you have fulfilled with your hand.” The pattern of divine speech → divine action reaches its apex in Christ who is both Word and Act — the speaking and the fulfilling are one person: “Long ago God spoke to our fathers by the prophets… but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son” (Hebrews 1:1-2).
25: The conditional promise to David: “You shall never lack a man to sit before me on the throne of Israel — if only your sons walk before me.” The conditionality of the Davidic promise (“if only”) is the problem Christ solves by obeying perfectly. Christ is the one Davidic descendant who walks before God with perfect integrity — the one who fulfills the condition the dynasty perpetually failed to meet. He is the promised king who sits on David’s throne not by hereditary succession alone but by personal righteousness.
26: “Let the word you spoke to your servant David be fulfilled.” Solomon prays for fulfillment of the Davidic covenant. The ultimate fulfillment is not the stone temple or any Davidic king before the exile, but Christ — the eternal Son enthroned at God’s right hand (Psalms 110:1; Acts 2:34-36; Hebrews 1:13). Every prayer for Davidic covenant fulfillment in the OT is a prayer — knowingly or not — for the coming of Jesus.
28: Solomon’s plea: “Give attention to your servant’s prayer and plea, O LORD, and listen to the cry and the prayer your servant brings before you today.” The great priestly prayer. Hebrews 5:7: “In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death.” Solomon’s prayer to the God who dwells in heaven is the type of Christ’s high-priestly intercession from the place of humiliation to the Father’s throne.
29: The prayer asks that God’s eyes be open toward “this house night and day — the place where you have said your name will dwell.” The name-theology of the temple (God’s name dwelling there, not God’s person in a contained way) prepares for Christ: “God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name” (Philippians 2:9). The divine name that dwelt in the temple now dwells in and as the person of the Son.
31: The first petition: when someone sins against a neighbor and takes an oath at the altar, God will judge and vindicate. The altar as the locus of covenant oath and divine judgment is the type of Christ as the place where sin is adjudicated. At the cross, God both “condemns the guilty by bringing his deed on his own head” (v32 — justice) and “vindicates the innocent by rewarding his righteousness” (v32 — grace). The cross is where both movements of v31-32 are fulfilled simultaneously.
32: “Condemn the guilty by bringing his deed on his own head, and vindicate the innocent by rewarding his righteousness.” At the cross, this double movement occurs: Christ the innocent is condemned, bearing guilty humanity’s deeds on his own head (2 Corinthians 5:21: “he made him to be sin who knew no sin”); and sinners are vindicated in Christ’s righteousness (Romans 5:18-19). The temple petition for just judgment is fulfilled at Calvary in inverted form: the innocent dies for the guilty so the guilty may be vindicated.
33: The second petition: when Israel is defeated by an enemy because of sin, if they turn back, confess God’s name, and pray toward this place, hear and forgive. The temple as the place of repentance-and-return establishes the pattern Christ fulfills: he is the place (John 2:21) to which all repentance is directed, the one in whose name forgiveness is declared (Acts 2:38; 1 Kings 4:12). Every OT prayer “toward this house” is, in retrospect, prayer toward Christ.
34: “Forgive the sin of your people Israel and bring them back to the land.” The petition pairs forgiveness and restoration — the double gift. Christ gives both: forgiveness of sins (Colossians 1:14) and the greater restoration, not to a piece of land, but to the new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17; Rev 21-22). The land promised to the fathers is a type whose ultimate antitype is the renewed cosmos “in which righteousness dwells” (2 Peter 3:13).
35: Third petition: when there is no rain because of sin, if the people pray toward this place and turn from sin, hear and forgive. Drought as covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:23-24) → prayer → forgiveness → rain. Christ is the living water (John 4:10; 1 Kings 7:37-38) who ends the deepest drought: “Whoever drinks of the water I will give him will never be thirsty again.” The rain for which Israel prays is a type of the Spirit Christ pours out (John 7:39; Acts 2:17-18).
36: “Teach them the right way to walk (ha-derekh ha-tovah) and send rain.” The connection between instruction in the right way and blessing resonates with Christ as the Way (John 14:6): he does not merely point to the right way — he is it. The Spirit sent by the risen Christ is both the teacher (John 14:26: “he will teach you all things”) and the one who brings the blessing of new life (Titus 3:5-6).
37: Fourth petition covers multiple disasters: famine, pestilence, blight, locusts, enemies besieging cities — the full range of covenant curse (Deut 28). The catalog of afflictions is the backdrop against which Christ’s liberating work stands out. He heals, casts out demons, stills storms, raises the dead — confronting each category of affliction directly in his ministry, demonstrating the arrival of the kingdom that reverses curse.
38: “Whatever prayer or plea is made by any person or by all your people Israel — each knowing the affliction of his own heart.” The phrase makkah levavo (the plague/affliction of his own heart) acknowledges that the deepest human wound is interior — sin within. The petition recognizes that external circumstances (famine, plague) are secondary to the inner corruption that generates them. Christ addresses this root: “For out of the heart come evil thoughts” (Matthew 15:19) — and Christ cleanses the heart (Acts 15:9: “cleansing their hearts by faith”).
39: “Give to each person according to his ways, whose heart you know — for you alone know the hearts of all the children of mankind.” God as the heart-knower (yode'a levavot) is affirmed. Christ, in his divine nature, knows what is in humans (John 2:24-25: “he knew all people — he knew what was in man”) — the same divine attribute. The prayer’s theological claim about God’s omniscience is a description of Christ’s nature recognized only in retrospect from the NT.
40: “So that they may fear you all the days they live in the land.” The goal of all divine response to prayer is covenant fear — the reverence that is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7). Christ both embodies this fear (Hebrews 5:7: “because of his reverent submission he was heard”) and produces it in his people through the Spirit: “I will put my Spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes” (Ezekiel 36:27).
41: Fifth petition: “Moreover, when a foreigner — someone not of your people Israel — comes from a distant land because of your name.” The inclusion of the nokri (foreigner) in Solomon’s prayer is remarkable — the temple is intended from its dedication as a house of prayer for all peoples. Jesus quotes this petition directly in cleansing the temple: “My house shall be called a house of prayer for all nations” (Mark 11:17, citing Isaiah 56:7). Christ’s indignation at the money changers is precisely because they excluded the Gentiles from the outer court where they should have been praying.
42: The foreigner comes “because of your great name and your mighty, outstretched arm.” The name and the powerful acts that undergird it draw the nations. In the NT, it is the name of Jesus that draws the Gentiles: “in his name the nations will hope” (Matthew 12:21, citing Isaiah 42:4). The outstretched arm — the Exodus image of divine deliverance — is enacted in the resurrection and exaltation of Christ, the ultimate mighty act that draws all nations.
43: “Hear in heaven your dwelling place and grant everything for which the foreigner prays — so that all peoples of the earth may know your name.” The purpose: all peoples knowing God’s name. The Great Commission is the NT form of this purpose: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations… teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you” (Matthew 28:19-20). Solomon’s prayer for the nations reaching God through the temple finds its fulfillment in the nations reaching God through Christ, the true temple.
44: Sixth petition: when Israel goes to war, if they pray toward Jerusalem and the temple, hear and uphold their cause. The warrior-prayer toward the holy city is the type of intercession in the name of Christ: “Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son” (John 14:13). Prayer “toward this house” has become prayer “in this name” — the place replaced by the person.
45: “Hear their prayer and plea in heaven and uphold their cause (mishpat).” The word mishpat (justice/cause) points to divine advocacy. Christ is the believer’s advocate (paraklētos — 1 John 2:1): “If anyone sins, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous one.” His intercession in heaven upholds the cause of his people before the divine tribunal.
46: Seventh and climactic petition: “If they sin against you — for there is no one who does not sin (ki ein adam asher lo yecheta).” The universal sinfulness that makes the seventh petition necessary is the theological premise of the gospel. Paul grounds the doctrine in this universal reality (Romans 3:23: “all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God”) and in Solomon’s assertion (Ecclesiastes 7:20). Christ alone is the exception — the one human who does not sin (Hebrews 4:15) — and therefore the only possible mediator for those who do.
47: In exile, if the people “come to their senses and repent” (shavu el-libam — return to their hearts) and pray. The prodigal son’s “he came to himself” (Luke 15:17) is the NT parallel: eis heauton de elthōn — coming to himself. The pattern of exile-repentance-return that Solomon prays for is the pattern Christ enacts in the parable and in salvation: the far country, the coming to oneself, the return to the Father who runs to meet the returning one.
48: “If they return to you with all their heart and soul in the land of their enemies.” Whole-hearted return from captivity is what Deuteronomy promises (Deuteronomy 30:1-10) and what the new covenant effects: “I will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring, so that you will love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul” (Deuteronomy 30:6). The return “with all their heart” is not achievable by command alone — it requires the Spirit’s work (Ezekiel 36:26-27), which Christ sends (John 16:7).
49: “Hear their prayer and plea from heaven.” The upward movement of the prayer — from the land of captivity, through the direction of Jerusalem, to heaven — mirrors the ascending mediatorial structure of Christ’s intercession. Hebrews 7:25: “He is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them.” The prayer heard from earth to heaven, and the answer flowing from heaven to earth, is the shape of all Christian prayer in Christ’s name.
50: “Forgive your people who have sinned against you and all their transgressions, and move their captors to show them compassion.” The double request — forgiveness from God and compassion from enemies — corresponds to the double work of the gospel: vertical reconciliation (forgiveness) and horizontal reconciliation (enemies becoming neighbors). Ephesians 2:14-16: Christ “has made us both one and has broken down the dividing wall of hostility… reconciling us both to God in one body through the cross.”
51: “For they are your people, your own inheritance, whom you brought out of Egypt, out of that iron-smelting furnace.” Israel as God’s possession (nachalah) brought from the furnace of Egypt — the Exodus as the paradigm of redemption. Christ enacts the new Exodus (Luke 9:31 — Moses and Elijah speak of his exodon) bringing a people out of a greater bondage: “He has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son” (Colossians 1:13).
52: “May your eyes be open to your servant’s plea and to your people Israel’s plea, listening whenever they call on you.” The final petition of Solomon’s prayer: continual divine attentiveness to prayer. Christ ensures this: “Ask and it will be given to you… For everyone who asks receives” (Matthew 7:7-8). The open divine eye Solomon prays for is guaranteed in Christ’s name: “Whatever you ask the Father in my name, he will give it to you” (John 16:23).
53: “For you separated them from all the peoples of the earth to be your inheritance, as you declared through your servant Moses.” Israel’s election as God’s separated people (hivdaltem) is the basis of the prayer. The NT widens the circle of the separated people: “You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession” (1 Peter 2:9 — addressed to Gentile believers). What was Israel by ethnic-covenantal identity, the church becomes through union with Christ.
54: When Solomon finished praying, he rose from kneeling before the altar with hands spread toward heaven. The bodily posture of prayer — kneeling, hands spread — mirrors what the Psalms prescribe and what Christ himself embodied: “Falling on his face he prayed” (Matthew 26:39). Solomon’s prayer-posture is the human posture before the divine; Christ’s prayer in Gethsemane is the perfect enactment of that posture in the face of the cross.
55: Solomon stood and blessed the whole assembly with a loud voice — the benedictory act after the completion of the great prayer. The risen Christ’s final act before ascension is blessing: “While he blessed them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven” (Luke 24:51). The temple dedication ends in blessing; the new covenant begins with the same.
56: “Blessed be the LORD, who has given rest to his people Israel in keeping with all his promises. Not one word has fallen to the ground.” The fulfillment of every divine word is the note Solomon strikes at the dedication. Hebrews 4:8-10 develops the rest-theme: Joshua gave temporary rest; Christ gives the ultimate Sabbath rest. And 2 Corinthians 1:20: “All the promises of God find their Yes in him” — the not-one-word-fallen principle reaches its ultimate affirmation in Christ.
57: “May the LORD our God be with us as he was with our fathers. May he never abandon or forsake us.” The petition “do not forsake us” (al-ya'azvenu) is answered definitively in Christ: “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matthew 28:20). The Emmanuel promise (Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:23 — God with us) ensures what Solomon prayed for: permanent, unbreakable divine presence with his people through the Spirit (John 14:16-17).
58: “That he may incline our hearts toward him, to walk in all his ways.” The prayer acknowledges the need for God to incline (lehattot) the human heart — it does not incline itself toward God. This is the doctrine of sovereign grace: the new covenant fulfills this prayer through the Spirit. Ezekiel 36:26-27: “I will give you a new heart” — and Christ sends the Spirit who produces this inclination (Philippians 2:13: “for it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure”).
59: “May these words of mine with which I have prayed before the LORD remain near to the LORD our God day and night.” Solomon prays that his prayer not be forgotten but remain before God perpetually. Christ lives to make perpetual intercession (Hebrews 7:25) — his prayers are not completed and forgotten but ongoing, continuous advocacy. The “day and night” presence of prayer before God that Solomon requests is what Christ’s eternal intercession guarantees.
60: “So that all the peoples of the earth may know that the LORD is God — there is no other.” The universal scope of Israel’s mission climaxes here: the temple is built not for Israel alone but so that all nations might know the only God. Christ commissions his disciples to this same universal task: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:19). The monotheistic proclamation “there is no other” is the theological foundation of both the Shema and the gospel.
61: “May your heart be wholly devoted to the LORD our God.” The exhortation to wholehearted devotion is the Deuteronomic ideal (Deuteronomy 6:5) — the first and greatest commandment. Jesus cites it as such (Mark 12:30). The church is called to this by the Spirit — and Christ alone has perfectly lived the whole-hearted devotion that he commands. His perfect love of God is the foundation on which our imperfect love is accepted.
62: The king and all Israel offered sacrifices before the LORD — the congregation joins in the priestly work of the dedication. The final book of Revelation shows the consummation of this: the redeemed from every nation worship before the throne (Revelation 7:9-10). Solomon’s assembly offering sacrifices is the OT form of the great eschatological assembly worshiping the Lamb who was slain.
63: Solomon offered 22,000 oxen and 120,000 sheep as peace offerings (shelamim) — the vast scale expressing the magnitude of dedication. The sheer number of sacrifices shows the inadequacy of animal sacrifice to finally accomplish what it points to: Hebrews 10:4, “for it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.” The 142,000 animals offered at the temple dedication are a down-payment on the one sacrifice Christ makes that does what all the others could not.
64: Solomon consecrates the middle of the court because the bronze altar was too small for all the offerings. The altar’s inadequacy — too small for the volume of worship — is an inadvertent parable: all the altars of the OT were too small for the sacrifice God required. The cross alone is the altar large enough, the place where the offering is finally adequate. Hebrews 13:10: “We have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat.”
65: The feast extends seven days, then seven more — a fourteen-day celebration from Lebo-hamath (the northern extent) to the Brook of Egypt (the southern). The geographic scope of the assembled worshipers (all of Israel from north to south) anticipates the universal scope of the new covenant community, gathered “from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Revelation 5:9).
66: The people bless the king and return home “full of joy and gladness for all the goodness the LORD had shown to his servant David and to his people Israel.” Joy and gladness (simchah ve-tov lev) are the covenant blessings of the festive assembly. The NT form: the disciples return to Jerusalem with great joy after Christ’s ascension (Luke 24:52); the early church ate together with “glad and generous hearts” (Acts 2:46). The dedication joy is a type of the post-resurrection, Spirit-given joy of those who know God has kept his greatest promise.
